title: SLAM Fundamentals
objective: Understand the SLAM problem, the major algorithmic approaches to solving it, and how SLAM enables autonomous robot navigation in environments where GPS is unavailable.
beginnerExplanation:
Imagine being dropped into a completely unfamiliar building with no map and no phone. You need to explore the building, figure out where everything is, and not get lost. You would naturally do what SLAM algorithms do — you would look around, note distinctive landmarks (a painting, a red door, a staircase), move forward while keeping track of how far you have walked, and gradually build a mental map of the building. If you returned to a place you recognised (the red door again!), you would correct any drift in your mental map — this is loop closure.

SLAM stands for Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping. It solves two problems at once: building a map of an unknown environment and tracking the robot's position within that map. This is a chicken-and-egg problem — you need a map to know where you are, but you need to know where you are to build a map. SLAM algorithms solve both simultaneously using probabilistic estimation.

The simplest form of SLAM uses a LiDAR sensor that scans the environment with a spinning laser, producing a 2D 'slice' of the surroundings at each scan. The algorithm matches consecutive scans to estimate how the robot moved (scan matching), and gradually stitches these scans together to build a complete 2D map. The result is an occupancy grid — a grid where each cell is marked as free space, occupied (wall), or unknown.

SLAM is what enables a robot vacuum to methodically clean every room without you providing a floor plan. It is what enables underground mining robots to navigate tunnel networks where GPS does not work. And it is the foundation upon which autonomous navigation is built.
deeperExplanation:
The SLAM problem is fundamentally one of state estimation under uncertainty. The robot's state (position and orientation) and the map (locations of landmarks or occupancy of cells) must be estimated jointly from noisy sensor measurements and imperfect motion models.

The earliest SLAM approaches used the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), maintaining a joint probability distribution over the robot's pose and all landmark positions. The state vector grows as new landmarks are discovered, and the covariance matrix captures correlations between landmark position estimates. When the robot revisits a known landmark, the covariance structure allows the entire map to be corrected simultaneously. EKF-SLAM works well for small environments with few landmarks but does not scale — the computational cost grows quadratically with the number of landmarks.

Particle filter SLAM (FastSLAM) uses a different approach. It represents the robot's trajectory as a set of particles — each particle is a hypothesis about the robot's path. Each particle carries its own map. This factorisation exploits the fact that, given a known robot trajectory, landmark positions are conditionally independent. FastSLAM scales better than EKF-SLAM but requires many particles for accurate estimates in large environments.

Modern SLAM systems are predominantly graph-based. The robot's trajectory is represented as a series of poses (nodes), connected by constraints (edges) derived from odometry and sensor measurements. When the robot recognises a previously visited location (loop closure), a new constraint is added connecting the current pose to the earlier one. The entire graph is then optimised to find the set of poses that best satisfies all constraints simultaneously. Libraries like g2o, GTSAM, and Ceres Solver perform this optimisation efficiently, handling thousands of poses in real time.

Visual SLAM (V-SLAM) uses cameras instead of or in addition to LiDAR. ORB-SLAM3 is the current state of the art for feature-based visual SLAM — it tracks ORB features across frames, triangulates 3D points, and optimises a graph of keyframes. LSD-SLAM and DSO are direct methods that work with pixel intensities rather than extracted features. Visual-inertial SLAM fuses camera data with IMU measurements for more robust tracking.

In Australian mining, SLAM is critical for underground operations. Emesent, a Brisbane-based company, developed Hovermap — an autonomous drone system that uses LiDAR SLAM to fly through underground mines, caves, and confined spaces, building detailed 3D maps without GPS. Their technology has been used in mines across Australia and globally, mapping stopes, tunnels, and voids that are too dangerous for human entry.
keyTakeaways: SLAM solves localisation and mapping simultaneously — essential where GPS is unavailable
Occupancy grids represent the map as free, occupied, or unknown cells
Loop closure — recognising a previously visited location — is critical for map consistency
Modern SLAM uses graph-based optimisation to maintain consistent maps over large areas
Visual SLAM uses cameras; LiDAR SLAM uses laser scanners; both have strengths and limitations
Australian companies like Emesent are global leaders in SLAM for underground and confined spaces
example: Emesent's Hovermap drone is deployed in an underground gold mine in Western Australia. The drone flies autonomously through a network of tunnels and stopes, with no GPS and no prior map. A spinning LiDAR (Velodyne VLP-16) captures 300,000 3D points per second. The onboard SLAM algorithm matches consecutive LiDAR scans using ICP (Iterative Closest Point), estimates the drone's motion, and builds a detailed 3D point cloud map in real time. When the drone returns to an area it has already mapped, the loop closure system detects the overlap and corrects any accumulated drift, snapping the map into geometric consistency. After a 15-minute flight, the result is a centimetre-accurate 3D map of the mine void — information that geologists and mining engineers use for stope stability assessment and ore body modelling. The entire process would take a survey team days using traditional methods.
commonMistakes: Assuming SLAM gives you a perfect map — SLAM maps always have uncertainty and can contain errors, especially in repetitive environments
Ignoring loop closure — without it, drift accumulates and the map becomes inconsistent over long traversals
Using visual SLAM in featureless environments (blank corridors, empty fields) where there are not enough visual features to track
Confusing SLAM with localisation — localisation in a known map is much simpler than building the map from scratch
Underestimating computational requirements — real-time SLAM on large environments requires significant processing power
exercise: Download and install the Cartographer ROS 2 package (Google's open-source SLAM library). Use one of the provided sample datasets (a LiDAR bag file recorded in a building). Run Cartographer in offline mode and visualise the resulting 2D occupancy grid map in RViz. Experiment with parameters: what happens if you reduce the scan matching resolution? What happens if you disable loop closure? Compare maps with and without loop closure to understand its importance. If ROS 2 installation is not feasible, use an online SLAM visualisation tool to explore the concepts.

quiz.question: What is the 'chicken-and-egg' problem that SLAM solves?
options: Whether to build hardware or software first
You need a map to localise, but you need to be localised to build a map
Whether to use LiDAR or cameras
How to move and sense at the same time
quiz.explanation: SLAM solves the fundamental interdependency between localisation and mapping. To build an accurate map, you need to know where the robot was when each measurement was taken. But to know where the robot is, you need a map to localise against. SLAM solves both problems jointly using probabilistic estimation techniques.

quiz.question: What is loop closure in SLAM and why is it important?
options: Closing the robot's control loop for better motor accuracy
Detecting when the robot has returned to a previously visited location, allowing accumulated drift to be corrected
Shutting down the SLAM algorithm when the robot returns to its start position
Closing gaps in the map where sensor data was missing
quiz.explanation: As a robot moves and builds a map, small errors in motion estimation accumulate (drift), causing the map to become geometrically inconsistent over time. Loop closure detects when the robot revisits a location it has mapped before, providing a constraint that allows the optimiser to correct the accumulated drift and produce a globally consistent map.

perspectives.slamResearcher: As a SLAM researcher, you develop new algorithms that push the boundaries of robustness, accuracy, and computational efficiency. You work with challenging datasets — underground mines, dense forests, dynamic urban environments.
perspectives.fieldRoboticist: As a field roboticist, you deploy SLAM systems in real environments and deal with the practical challenges: sensor mounting, dust on lenses, featureless corridors, dynamic objects, and limited compute on battery-powered platforms.
perspectives.miningEngineer: As a mining engineer, SLAM gives you detailed 3D models of underground voids and tunnels that inform extraction planning, stability assessment, and safety management — data that was previously expensive and dangerous to obtain.
